agent_output:
# Domain Analysis — E-Commerce Order & Inventory Platform

---

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This encodes the **order lifecycle** of a typical e-commerce platform, spanning from product browsing through cart management, checkout with inventory reservation, payment confirmation, and post-delivery refunds. The rules govern the interplay between **inventory management**, **pricing/promotions**, **checkout flow**, and **post-sale policy** — four distinct but interconnected subdomains.

### Ubiquitous Language

| Term | Precise Definition |
|---|---|
| **Cart** | A transient, uncommitted collection of line items associated with a customer session. Holds no inventory guarantees. |
| **Inventory Reservation** | A time-bound hold placed on physical inventory units when a customer initiates checkout. Prevents overselling during the payment window. |
| **Checkout** | The transition from cart to order-in-progress. Triggers inventory reservation and begins the 15-minute payment window. |
| **Reservation TTL** | The 15-minute window from checkout initiation within which payment must be completed; expiration releases reserved inventory. |
| **Abandoned Checkout** | A checkout where no payment confirmation is received within the Reservation TTL. |
| **Active Promotion** | A pricing rule currently applied to a product (e.g., 20% off). A product may have at most one active promotion at any time. |
| **Discount Code** | A customer-supplied code that applies a price reduction at the order level. Mutually exclusive with promotional pricing. |
| **Discount Mechanism** | The generalized concept encompassing both promotions and discount codes. Only one mechanism may apply per order. |
| **Payment Confirmed** | The irreversible state transition after which an order becomes immutable. Represents successful payment processing. |
| **Order Immutability** | The invariant that no modifications (items, quantities, addresses, pricing) are permitted after payment confirmation. |
| **Zero Inventory** | A product state where available units = 0. The product may remain listed (visible) but is unpurchasable. |
| **Refund Window** | The 30-calendar-day period following delivery date during which a customer may request a refund. |
| **Delivery Date** | The confirmed date the order was delivered to the customer; serves as the anchor for the refund window. |

### Bounded Context

This analysis spans **four bounded contexts**:

1. **Cart Context** — item collection, no commitments
2. **Inventory Context** — stock levels, reservations, reservation lifecycle
3. **Pricing Context** — promotions, discount codes, discount exclusivity
4. **Order Context** — checkout, payment, immutability, refund policy

---

## Business Rules

| # | Rule | Invariant | Testable Assertion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **BR-1** | Inventory is NOT reserved when items are added to a cart; reservation occurs only at checkout initiation. | ✅ true | Adding an item to a cart does not decrement available inventory or create any reservation record. |
| **BR-2** | An inventory reservation has a TTL of exactly 15 minutes from checkout initiation. | ✅ true | A reservation created at time T must be automatically released if no payment is confirmed by T+15min. |
| **BR-3** | When a reservation expires (abandoned checkout), all reserved units are returned to available inventory. | ✅ true | After TTL expiration, available inventory increases by the exact quantity that was reserved, and the reservation record transitions to a "released" state. |
| **BR-4** | A product may have at most one active promotion at any given time. | ✅ true | Attempting to activate a second promotion on a product that already has an active promotion must fail or deactivate the existing one first. |
| **BR-5** | Discount codes and promotional pricing are mutually exclusive per order — only one discount mechanism applies. | ✅ true | An order with a promotional price applied must reject any discount code; an order with a discount code applied must use the base price (not the promotional price) for the discounted items, OR reject the code if a promo is active. |
| **BR-6** | Orders cannot be modified after payment is confirmed. | ✅ true | Any mutation request (item change, quantity change, address change, cancel) on an order in "paid" or later status must be rejected. |
| **BR-7** | A product with zero available inventory cannot be purchased, regardless of listing visibility. | ✅ true | Checkout initiation (reservation attempt) for a product with 0 available units must fail, even if the product detail page is accessible. |
| **BR-8** | Refund requests must be submitted within 30 calendar days of the delivery date. | ✅ true | A refund request submitted on delivery_date + 31 days must be rejected. A request on delivery_date + 30 days must be accepted (if otherwise valid). |

### Critical Invariant Notes

**BR-1 + BR-7 create a race condition window.** Between "add to cart" and "begin checkout," another customer may purchase the last unit. This is by design — the business accepts cart-level disappointment in exchange for not holding phantom reservations. The system MUST handle the case where checkout initiation fails due to insufficient inventory gracefully.

**BR-5 requires a clear precedence rule** not stated in the requirements. When a customer applies a discount code to a cart containing a product with an active promotion, the system must decide: (a) reject the discount code, (b) reject the promotional price and apply the code, or (c) apply whichever is more favorable to the customer. **This is an ambiguity that needs stakeholder clarification.** For this analysis, I assume the system rejects the discount code and applies the promotional pricing, since promotions are merchant-initiated and should take precedence.

---

## Acceptance Criteria

### AC-1: Cart does not reserve inventory

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | A product with 5 units of available inventory |
| **When** | A customer adds 3 units of the product to their cart |
| **Then** | Available inventory remains 5; no reservation record is created |

### AC-2: Checkout reserves inventory

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | A product with 5 available units, and a cart containing 3 units of that product |
| **When** | The customer initiates checkout |
| **Then** | Available inventory decreases to 2; a reservation record is created for 3 units with an expiration time of now + 15 minutes |

### AC-3: Abandoned checkout releases reservation

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | An active reservation for 3 units created at 10:00:00 |
| **When** | The clock reaches 10:15:00 with no payment confirmed |
| **Then** | The reservation is released; available inventory increases by 3; the checkout session is marked as abandoned |

### AC-4: Payment within TTL completes the order

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | An active reservation for 3 units created at 10:00:00 |
| **When** | Payment is confirmed at 10:14:59 |
| **Then** | The reservation converts to a committed allocation; the order transitions to "paid" status; inventory is permanently decremented |

### AC-5: Single active promotion per product

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | Product X has an active promotion "Summer Sale 20% off" |
| **When** | A merchant attempts to activate a second promotion "Flash Deal 30% off" on Product X |
| **Then** | The operation is rejected with an error indicating an active promotion already exists, OR the existing promotion is deactivated first (business decision needed) |

### AC-6: Discount code rejected when promotion is active

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | A cart containing Product X, which has an active promotion applied |
| **When** | The customer applies discount code "SAVE10" |
| **Then** | The discount code is rejected; the order total reflects only the promotional pricing |

### AC-7: Order immutability after payment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | An order in "paid" status |
| **When** | Any modification request is submitted (change item, change quantity, change address, cancel) |
| **Then** | The request is rejected with an error indicating the order is immutable post-payment |

### AC-8: Zero inventory blocks purchase

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | A product with 0 available inventory that is still listed on the storefront |
| **When** | A customer attempts to initiate checkout with this product in their cart |
| **Then** | Checkout initiation fails with a clear message that the product is out of stock |

### AC-9: Refund within 30-day window accepted

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | An order delivered on March 1st |
| **When** | A refund is requested on March 31st (day 30) |
| **Then** | The refund request is accepted for processing |

### AC-10: Refund outside 30-day window rejected

| | |
|---|---|
| **Given** | An order delivered on March 1st |
| **When** | A refund is requested on April 1st (day 31) |
| **Then** | The refund request is rejected with a message indicating the refund window has expired |

### Edge Cases

| Scenario | Expected Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Concurrent checkout for last unit** | Two customers attempt checkout for the last unit simultaneously. Exactly one reservation succeeds; the other fails with an out-of-stock error. No overselling. |
| **Payment arrives at exactly 15:00.000** | The boundary condition: if payment timestamp equals reservation_created + 15min exactly, this must be explicitly defined as accepted OR rejected. Recommend: **accepted** (≤ 15 min). |
| **Cart contains mix of promoted and non-promoted items + discount code** | Discount code is rejected for the entire order (not selectively applied to non-promoted items), per BR-5's "per order" scope. |
| **Product inventory drops to zero while customer is in checkout** | Reservation was already placed at checkout initiation — the customer's reservation is honored. Inventory cannot go negative. |
| **Refund requested for undelivered order** | The 30-day window has not started. Refund rule does not apply — this should route to a separate cancellation/dispute process. |
| **Promotion deactivated mid-checkout** | If the promotional price was captured at checkout initiation, the customer should receive the promotional price. Price should be snapshotted at reservation time. |
| **Multiple discount codes entered** | Only one discount mechanism applies — second code entry must be rejected or replace the first. |
| **Reservation released, customer then pays** | Payment must fail. The reservation is no longer valid; inventory may have been claimed by another customer. The system must validate reservation status before accepting payment. |

---

## Business Impact

| Dimension | Assessment |
|---|---|
| **Financial** | The "reserve at checkout, not at cart" model maximizes inventory availability and reduces phantom holds, directly improving conversion rates. The 15-minute TTL balances customer experience with inventory liquidity. The single-discount-mechanism rule simplifies margin calculations and prevents discount stacking that could erode margins. |
| **Operational** | Reservation TTL management requires a reliable scheduler or event-driven expiration mechanism — clock skew and distributed timer failures are operational risks. The immutability rule simplifies order processing pipelines but pushes complexity to customer support (modification requests become cancellation + reorder). |
| **User** | Customers may experience frustration when items in their cart become unavailable at checkout (BR-1 + BR-7 gap). The discount exclusivity rule may confuse customers who expect to combine a sale price with a coupon. Clear UX messaging is critical. |
| **Risk** | **Overselling** is the highest-severity risk if reservation logic fails. **Stale price snapshots** are a risk if promotions change during checkout. **Refund window disputes** will arise around delivery date accuracy — if delivery tracking is unreliable, the 30-day clock becomes contentious. |

### Stakeholders Affected

| Stakeholder Group | Impact |
|---|---|
| **Customers** | Cart-to-checkout inventory loss; discount limitation confusion; 30-day refund boundary disputes |
| **Merchandising/Marketing** | Single-promotion constraint limits stacking campaigns; must coordinate promotion scheduling |
| **Customer Support** | Post-payment immutability increases support volume for modification requests; refund window boundary cases require clear policy tooling |
| **Finance/Accounting** | Clean single-discount model simplifies revenue recognition; refund window provides predictable liability window |
| **Engineering/Ops** | Reservation TTL requires distributed timer reliability; concurrent checkout demands strong consistency guarantees on inventory state |

---

## Confidence Assessment

| | |
|---|---|
| **Confidence Level** | **82%** |

### High Confidence Areas
- Cart vs. checkout reservation semantics (BR-1, BR-2, BR-3) — clearly stated
- Order immutability after payment (BR-6) — unambiguous
- Zero-inventory purchase block (BR-7) — unambiguous
- Refund window duration (BR-8) — clearly stated
- Single promotion per product (BR-4) — clearly stated

### Low Confidence Areas
- **Discount code vs. promotion precedence** — the rules say they can't combine, but don't specify which wins. My analysis assumes promotion takes precedence; this MUST be confirmed with the product team.
- **Price snapshot timing** — rules don't specify when prices are locked in. Is it at cart addition, checkout initiation, or payment confirmation? This matters enormously for correctness.
- **Refund "requested" vs. "approved"** — the rule says refunds must be *requested* within 30 days. Does the 30-day clock govern request submission only, or must the refund also be *processed* within that window?
- **Partial order scenarios** — if a multi-item order has one item go out of stock at checkout, does the entire checkout fail or can a partial order proceed?
- **Reservation TTL boundary** — is 15 minutes inclusive or exclusive? (≤ vs. <)

### Assumptions Made
1. Promotion takes precedence over discount codes when they conflict (needs confirmation)
2. The 15-minute TTL is inclusive (payment at exactly T+15:00 is accepted)
3. Prices are snapshotted at checkout initiation (reservation time), not cart addition
4. The refund window is based on calendar days, not business days
5. "Delivery date" refers to confirmed delivery per tracking, not estimated delivery
6. Checkout is all-or-nothing — partial reservations are not supported
7. "Cannot be modified" includes all mutations: items, quantities, shipping address, and cancellation

---

## Rejection Reasons

No implementation to reject — this is a greenfield analysis. However, any implementation that exhibits the following behaviors **must be rejected**:

| Violation | Business Rule Broken |
|---|---|
| Inventory decremented on "add to cart" | BR-1: Reservation occurs only at checkout |
| Reservation persists beyond 15 minutes without payment | BR-2/BR-3: TTL must be enforced |
| Two active promotions on the same product | BR-4: Max one active promotion |
| Discount code and promotion both applied to same order | BR-5: Mutually exclusive discount mechanisms |
| Order line items or details changed after payment confirmed | BR-6: Post-payment immutability |
| Successful purchase of a zero-inventory product | BR-7: Zero inventory = unpurchasable |
| Refund accepted after 30 days post-delivery | BR-8: Refund window enforcement |
| Payment accepted on an expired/released reservation | BR-2/BR-3: Stale reservation must not convert to order |

# Domain Analysis — E-Commerce Order & Inventory Platform

---

## Domain Analysis

### Business Context

This encodes the **order lifecycle** of a typical e-commerce platform, spanning from product browsing through cart management, checkout with inventory reservation, payment confirmation, and post-delivery refunds. The rules govern the interplay between **inventory management**, **pricing/promotions**, **checkout flow**, and **post-sale policy** — four distinct but interconnected subdomains.
